lessons.title: Move, rotate, scale, colour
lessons.objectives:
I can add, delete, and move objects in Blender
I can scale and rotate objects
I can use a material to add colour to objects
I can make a 3D snowman model
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: object · sphere · cone · cylinder · move (G) · scale (S) · rotate (R) · delete (X) · material (colour). TA: everyone has Blender open in rendered mode. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–8) Starter — where is 3D animation? Pupils TICK on the starter worksheet where they see 3D animation (multi-select, no writing). Show the Agent 327 Blender film still; mention the green screen (replaced by 3D later).
   • Support: choose “objects” from a tick list.  • Core: name a film/game.  • Challenge: why a green screen?
2. (8–20) I-do → we-do. Delete the cube (X). Demo the four moves G/S/R/X on one object (I do); class names the key for each move (we do).
   • Support: the key for each move is a choice on the worksheet.  • Core: what a material does.  • Challenge: variables link. Likely error: scaling shoots off → fix-words “press Esc, move the mouse away from the centre, then S again.”
3. (20–40) You-do — build a snowman: two spheres (body + head), move/scale, a cone nose, rotate it. Steps are in order on the worksheet (drag-to-order).
   • Support: follow the ordered steps.  • Core: build unaided.  • Challenge: add a scarf/hat/arms (explorer). TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
4. (40–52) Colour with materials — make a material, name it, reuse it on both eyes + buttons. Label-a-diagram on the worksheet (name the shape of each snowman part). Keep a screenshot of the finished snowman.
5. (52–55) Plenary — pupils self-tick the 4 “I can…”; show their snowman. TA notes who used and reused a material.
Differentiation is on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound on slides; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Animation, names, parenting
lessons.objectives:
I can add, move, and delete keyframes to make an animation
I can play and move through my animation using the timeline
I can give my objects useful names
I can join objects together using parenting
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: keyframe (I) · tweening (in-between frames) · stop motion · timeline · frame · name · parenting. TA: open last lesson’s snow scene or the template scene. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — two kinds of animation. Pupils SORT cards into stop-motion vs keyframe (card-sort, no writing). Quick recall: which key deletes (X)?
   • Support: who fills the in-between frames? (the computer).
2. (7–17) Keyframes vs tweening. Optional teacher-played hook: ~10s of an old stop-motion clip (low-arousal, calm), then “why is keyframing faster on a computer?”. Label-a-diagram on the worksheet (keyframe vs tweened on the arc diagram).
3. (17–34) I-do → we-do → you-do — keyframing. Demo on the snow scene: select object, I at frame 1, move the timeline, move the object, I again, play (I do/we do). Pairs animate the snowball rolling into the snowman (you do). Steps are in order on the worksheet.
   • Support: ordered steps + key-choice questions.  • Core: predict what happens between two keyframes.  • Challenge: make the hat fall off / bounce (explorer). Likely error: no keyframe at the START, or object not selected → “select it, then keyframe at frame 1.” TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
4. (34–52) Names and parenting — name objects in the Outliner; parent head to body (drag + Shift). Worksheet: why clear names help; why parenting is useful. Keep a screenshot of the timeline.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 4 “I can…”; show the animation play. TA notes who named + parented.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Complex models and colours
lessons.objectives:
I can use edit mode and extrude
I can use loop cut and face editing
I can put different colours on different parts of one model
I can make a rocket model
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: edit mode (Tab) · vertex (corner) · edge (line) · face (flat surface) · extrude (E) · loop cut · material. TA: everyone has Blender open. Read the 4 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — real or computer made? Discuss that 3D models can look real. Pupils choose what a model is built from (vertices, edges, faces).
   • Support: the flat side is a face.  • Challenge: why build on a computer instead of filming?
2. (5–13) The building blocks — match vertex/edge/face to their meaning on the worksheet (matching grid). Show vertex/edge/face select in edit mode (Tab).
3. (13–37) I-do → we-do → you-do — edit mode + extrude. Demo: add a cylinder, Tab, select the top face, E to extrude the body, extrude the nose, scale in (I do/we do). Pupils make a rocket (you do); steps are in order on the worksheet. Loop cut adds an edge ring.
   • Support: ordered steps + key-choice (E = extrude).  • Core: what loop cut adds.  • Challenge (explorer): make a car. Likely error: tools missing → “you’re not in edit mode, press Tab”; extruding the wrong way → pull the mouse the right direction.
4. (37–52) Many colours on one model — in edit mode, select faces, pick a material, Assign. Three named materials (variables link). Coloured-rocket image on the worksheet. Keep a screenshot.
5. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; show the model. TA notes who extruded + used >1 material.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Organic modelling
lessons.objectives:
I can use proportional editing
I can use the knife tool
I can use subdivision
I can make a natural-looking model
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: organic · symmetry · proportional editing · knife tool · subdivision. TA: everyone has Blender open. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–5) Starter — name the parts. Pupils label vertex/edge/face on a model (label-a-diagram, near-zero writing).
   • Support: a vertex is a corner point.
2. (5–10) Real things are not perfect — show the two faces (symmetrical vs natural). Pupils choose that natural things are a little asymmetrical. Discuss why even models look fake.
3. (10–22) I-do → you-do — proportional editing. Turn it on, move one point, nearby points follow (like clay). Make a bendy tree trunk.
   • Support: choice “moves the points nearby”.  • Core: predict what nearby points do. Likely error: nothing bends → “turn up the strength.”
4. (22–40) The knife tool — cut a palm-leaf shape into a plane, Enter to fix, delete waste, bend with proportional editing. Steps are in order on the worksheet.
   • Support: ordered steps.  • Core: what subdivision does.  • Challenge (explorer): add coconuts / name + parent the tree parts. TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.”
5. (40–52) Subdivision — split faces to shape a smoother desert island. Palm-tree image on the worksheet. Keep a screenshot.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…” + a 1–5 confidence slider (uncredited) so the teacher sees who needs help next lesson.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Lights, camera, render
lessons.objectives:
I can add and edit lighting
I can set up the camera
I can compare different render modes
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: render · light (spot · sun · point) · power · camera · focus (f-stop) · rule of thirds · ray tracing. TA: share the template desert island scene. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–7) Starter — make a treasure chest. Pupils ORDER the steps to make a chest (recall earlier skills: cube, scale, extrude, materials).
   • Support: which key extrudes (E)?
2. (7–10) Lights or camera? — pupils SORT settings into Light vs Camera (card-sort).
3. (10–27) I-do → you-do — lights. Spot (cone) / sun (even) / point (all around). Demo a spotlight on the lighthouse, turn up the power, animate it spinning. Pupils add a light; steps are in order on the worksheet.
   • Support: a spotlight makes a cone of light.  • Likely error: light too weak → “turn up the power.”
4. (27–47) Camera + rule of thirds. Press 0 to look through the camera; place the subject on a third line; focus (f-stop) blurs the background.
   • Core: what the focus does.  • Challenge: when is slow ray tracing worth it vs the fast mode? TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” Keep a screenshot of the camera view.
5. (47–55) Render modes + plenary — fast preview vs ray tracing (Cycles). Self-tick the 3 “I can…” + a 1–5 confidence slider before the project lesson.
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound on slides.

lessons.title: Project
lessons.objectives:
I can plan and make a 3–10 second animation
I can render my animation to a video file
I can self- and peer-assess my work
lessons.outline:
Routine first. No new vocabulary — bring together the whole unit. TA: Blender open; pupils have their earlier checklists for the keys/tools. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud.
1. (0–3) Starter — how was this made? Show the bouncing-cube frame; pupils choose “keyframes on the timeline”. Open the file to check.
   • Support: a keyframe stores a position in time.
2. (3–8) Remember your skills — pupils SORT skills into Modelling / Animation / Lights & camera (card-sort). Then ORDER the production steps (model → name/parent → animate → lights/camera → render).
3. (8–30) You-do — make your film. Build models, animate, light, frame.
   • Support: a short simple scene is fine (3–10s); ordered steps on the worksheet.  • Core: write the plan (what happens).  • Challenge: use an advanced skill (knife tool / proportional editing / parented animation). TA: “prompt, do not do it for them.” Movement break halfway as routine. Keep a screenshot / the rendered file.
4. (30–45) Render to a video file; keep going on models/animation if needed (flexible timing — stop for a midway progress check instead of fixed times).
5. (45–52) Peer feedback — watch a partner’s film; write one thing that is great + one to improve.
6. (52–55) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…” + a 1–5 “how proud are you?” slider; what was most interesting/difficult, what next?
Differentiation on the SAME task; movement breaks routine; no flashing/sound on slides.
